lessons.title: Blobs, trees, commits
objectives: Explain the four Git object types and how they reference each other
Trace a branch ref down to the blobs it ultimately points at

blocks.markdown:
## Git is a content-addressed database

Everything in `.git/objects` is one of four object types, keyed by the SHA-1 of its content:

- **blob** — file contents (no name, no mode)
- **tree** — a directory: names + modes pointing at blobs/trees
- **commit** — a tree + parents + author + message
- **tag** — an annotated pointer

Branches are just files containing a commit hash. That's the whole trick.

blocks.mermaid: graph TD; HEAD-->main; main-->C2[commit C2]; C2-->C1[commit C1]; C2-->T2[tree]; T2-->B1[blob README.md]; T2-->T3[tree src/]; T3-->B2[blob main.ts];
blocks.caption: A commit points at a tree; trees point at blobs and other trees.

blocks.markdown: `git cat-file -p <hash>` pretty-prints **any** object. It's the single best tool for building intuition — poke at real objects in a real repo as you go.

options.text: A copy of the working directory
A file containing a commit hash
A linked list stored in the index

blocks.instructions: In any Git repo, run `git cat-file -t HEAD`. What object type does it print? (Type exactly what the command outputs.)

blocks.label: Fuzzy on trees vs the index? Talk it through with your agent.

context.note: Focus on the object model as a graph, not on porcelain commands.

blocks.markdown: Plumbing commands (`hash-object`, `update-ref`, `cat-file`) **will let you corrupt a repo**. Practice in a throwaway directory, not in your dotfiles.

blocks.source:
mkdir scratch && cd scratch && git init
echo 'hello metis' | git hash-object -w --stdin
# → writes a blob, prints its full 40-char hash
git log --oneline -1   # after your first commit

blocks.instructions: Create a scratch repo, make one commit, then paste the output of `git log --oneline -1` (short hash + message).
Push your scratch repo to GitHub (public) and link a commit where you created a blob with `hash-object`. Evidence, not vibes.
Read the "Git Internals — Git Objects" chapter of Pro Git (10.2). No proof needed — this one's on your honor.
